stories.headline: Motorcyclist killed in solo crash on I-25 near Park Avenue in Denver
stories.summary:
One person died Monday afternoon after a motorcycle crash on Interstate 25 near Park Avenue in Denver, according to the Denver Police Department. No other vehicles were involved in the collision.

Authorities announced the fatality on the department's social media account shortly after 4 p.m. The HOV lane was blocked as investigators worked the scene, causing delays for commuters in the area.

Police did not immediately release the identity of the victim or additional details about the circumstances of the crash. Motorists were advised to expect significant delays while the investigation continued.

stories.headline: 33-year-old woman shot and killed while driving near Aurora apartment complex
stories.summary:
A 33-year-old woman was shot and killed Saturday in Aurora after being struck by a single gunshot while driving through a parking lot near the Weatherstone Apartments in the 15500 block of East 12th Avenue, near the intersection of Chambers Road and Colfax Avenue.

The victim drove herself to a nearby business after the shooting before collapsing inside. She was transported to a hospital with life-threatening injuries and was unable to provide information to investigators before she died. The Arapahoe County Coroner's Office had not yet publicly identified her.

Aurora police said no suspect had been identified. Officers noted a bullet hole in the victim's vehicle. Anyone with information is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Denver man sentenced to 35 years in prison for 2025 shooting death
stories.summary:
Giovanni Anthony Gonzalez, 19, was sentenced Friday to 35 years in the Colorado Department of Corrections after pleading guilty to second-degree murder in the January 2025 shooting death of Aaron Roybal, 52, in Denver. Denver District Court Judge Martin Foster Egelhoff imposed the sentence during the same hearing in which Gonzalez entered the plea.

The fatal shooting occurred around 9 p.m. on January 12, 2025, near South Sheridan Boulevard and West Wyoming Place. Security cameras recorded Gonzalez pulling a U-turn outside a cannabis dispensary and driving back to Roybal's vehicle before shooting him as Roybal exited the car.

As part of the plea deal, a first-degree murder charge was dropped and a jury trial scheduled for May 4 was canceled. The agreement resolved one of Denver's 2025 homicide cases through the court system.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers fast-track bill to rename César Chávez state holiday amid sexual abuse allegations
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators are swiftly advancing a bill to rename the March 31 state holiday currently honoring labor leader César Chávez, after Chávez was accused last week of sexually abusing girls and women. The House State, Civic, Military, and Veterans Affairs Committee passed the bill unanimously Monday, with all but two state representatives signing on as co-sponsors.

The legislation is expected to be signed into law before the March 31 observance, which serves as an optional holiday for Colorado state agencies. The bill would rename the holiday to honor farmworkers broadly rather than tying it to Chávez specifically.

The move follows an announcement last week by Denver Mayor Mike Johnston that the city would also change a full municipal holiday named for Chávez and strip his name from a city park. Denver City Council President Amanda Sandoval has been meeting with community leaders to determine new names for the park and other city spaces.

stories.headline: Denver airport officials say no ICE deployment planned at DIA as agents appear at major U.S. hubs
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport officials said Monday that security screening was operating normally and that they had received no communication indicating Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents would staff its checkpoints. The statement came as armed federal immigration officers in tactical gear appeared at several of the nation's busiest airports following a directive from President Donald Trump during the ongoing partial government shutdown.

DIA was not among the 13 airports on a list obtained by CNN where ICE deployments were expected Monday. Security wait times at the airport's East and West checkpoints ranged from zero to 20 minutes at various points during the day, a contrast with hours-long delays reported at some other airports.

The deployments raised questions nationally about the scope of immigration enforcement at domestic travel checkpoints. ICE's Denver field office did not respond to a request for comment. The broader government funding lapse, which began February 14, has left hundreds of thousands of Homeland Security workers including TSA officers working without full pay.

stories.headline: Broncos' Burnham Yard stadium plans hinge on solving major traffic and parking challenges
stories.summary:
Traffic management, parking and transit access have emerged as the top concerns from residents near the Burnham Yard site in west Denver where the Denver Broncos plan to build a new stadium by 2031, according to community meeting feedback compiled by the city and team. The La Alma Lincoln Park Neighborhood Association said transportation is the No. 1 issue raised by neighbors.

The Broncos have released early transportation details including proposed changes to street crossings with RTD's light rail tracks, potential parking options and connections to pedestrian and bike infrastructure. The team's conceptual plan calls for routing event traffic through the north and south ends of the banana-shaped rail yard site rather than through adjacent residential areas.

The project envisions more than just a stadium — the Broncos plan a mixed-use entertainment district with restaurants, open space and potentially hotels or apartments for year-round use. The site is bounded roughly by West 13th Avenue and West Sixth Avenue, with freight rail to the west and RTD light rail to the east. Residents expressed concern that proposed solutions, such as altering neighborhood streets, could harm the surrounding community.

stories.headline: Denver rents post one of steepest declines in the country over the past year
stories.summary:
New data shows the Denver metro area recorded one of the highest rent decreases in the nation over the past year, according to an Axios Denver report. The figures reflect a significant shift in Denver's housing market after years of rapid rent increases.

The decline has prompted discussions among city officials and community leaders about Denver's housing landscape. Denver City Council President Amanda Sandoval has been among those meeting with stakeholders to address related neighborhood concerns.

The data signals continued pressure on landlords in the metro area to offer incentives to attract and retain tenants, a trend that has been building as new apartment supply has grown across the Denver region.

stories.headline: Liberty Energy loses 330-megawatt Texas data center power contract
stories.summary:
Denver-based Liberty Energy lost a contract to supply 330 megawatts of power to a Texas data center project after the developer modified and delayed the expansion, according to the Denver Business Journal. The deal's collapse represents a setback for the oilfield services company's push into the data center power market.

Liberty Energy, headquartered in Denver, has been expanding its energy services offerings beyond its core fracking business, and the data center sector had been seen as a growth opportunity. The circumstances surrounding the developer's decision to alter and postpone the project were not detailed.

The lost contract is likely to draw attention from investors monitoring Liberty Energy's diversification strategy as demand for data center power infrastructure continues to grow nationally.

stories.headline: Fire at Denver recycling facility near I-70 sends smoke plume across metro area
stories.summary:
A fire broke out Monday morning at a recycling facility near East 48th Avenue and Colorado Boulevard in Denver, close to Interstate 70, sending a large smoke plume visible across much of the metro area. Aerial footage showed a pile of cardboard burning at the property.

The blaze prompted concerns among residents and commuters in the area near the facility. Emergency responders were dispatched to the scene, though the extent of damage to the facility was not immediately reported.

The fire added to air quality concerns in a week already marked by extremely dry and windy conditions and elevated fire danger across the Front Range.

stories.headline: More record-breaking heat expected in Denver this week with critical fire danger by midweek
stories.summary:
After a brief temperature dip Sunday, another wave of record-breaking heat is forecast for the Denver metro area this week, with highs climbing into the mid-to-upper 80s Tuesday and Wednesday and some areas potentially approaching 90 degrees. The National Weather Service in Boulder said all-time March records could be challenged again by Wednesday.

Alongside the heat, conditions are expected to turn increasingly dry with gusty winds, producing elevated to critical fire weather concerns across much of the region by midweek. Additional Red Flag Warnings are likely Thursday along the Front Range as low humidity and strong gusts combine with unseasonably warm temperatures.

A cold front is expected to bring a brief cool-down by Friday, though temperatures will remain above normal. Another warming trend is forecast heading into the following weekend, prolonging the dangerous stretch of fire weather for Colorado.

stories.headline: RTD adds rail capacity, hosts tailgate for Denver Summit FC inaugural home opener Saturday
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District is adding extra rail cars to the D, E and W lines and boosting security for fans attending Denver Summit FC's first-ever home game Saturday at Empower Field at Mile High. The noon kickoff against the Washington Spirit is expected to draw large crowds for the new women's soccer franchise's inaugural home match.

RTD is hosting a tailgate from 10 a.m. to 11:30 a.m. outside the Chestnut Pavilion at Denver Union Station for E Line riders, offering treats, giveaways and wayfinding assistance. Auraria West Station will close at 10 a.m. due to expected crowd volumes; fans are directed to use Empower Field at Mile High Station instead, which will reopen about an hour after the game.

The agency encouraged fans to use the MyRide app for Day Passes or contactless Tap-n-Ride payment to avoid ticket vending machine lines. Transit Police and security will have an increased presence at stations and stops near the stadium. Riders can also download the Transit Watch app to report safety concerns.

stories.headline: Nuggets return to full strength as Peyton Watson sparks 128-112 win over Trail Blazers
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets defeated the Portland Trail Blazers 128-112 Sunday, with Peyton Watson making his return from a six-week hamstring absence to give the team its complete roster for the first time since November 12. Watson scored 14 points, grabbed six defensive rebounds and added three assists in 20 minutes of play.

Nikola Jokic recorded his 35th triple-double of the season with 22 points, 14 rebounds and 14 assists, while Jamal Murray also scored 22. Cam Johnson contributed 19 points on 7-of-10 shooting, and all five starters finished in double figures. Denver outscored Portland 27-9 in fast-break points and 54-46 in the paint.

The win improved Denver's record to 44-28, keeping the Nuggets in fifth place in the Western Conference with a half-game lead over Minnesota for that seed and within striking distance of Houston for fourth. Ten regular-season games remain as the team prepares for the playoffs with its full complement of players available for the first time this season.

weather.notable: About 28 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: There's no better feeling in the world than a warm pizza box on your lap.

topStory.headline: ICE Agents Deployed to Major U.S. Airports to Assist Understaffed TSA During Partial Government Shutdown
topStory.summary:
Armed federal immigration officers in tactical gear were deployed to terminals at some of the busiest airports in the United States on Monday, standing near security lines and checkpoints after President Donald Trump ordered the move to address severe staffing shortages at the Transportation Security Administration. ICE agents and Department of Homeland Security personnel were confirmed at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International, John F. Kennedy International in New York, Newark Liberty International in New Jersey, O'Hare International in Chicago, George Bush Intercontinental in Houston, Louis Armstrong International in New Orleans, Philadelphia International, and Pittsburgh International, among others, with White House border czar Tom Homan saying agents were deployed to at least 14 airports nationwide.

TSA officers have been working without pay for weeks following Congress's failure to renew DHS funding, triggering a partial government shutdown driven by a partisan stalemate over immigration policy. The national TSA callout rate reached 11.76% on Sunday, with some airports recording dramatically higher rates — New Orleans at 42.3%, Atlanta at 41.5%, and JFK at 37.4% — contributing to security lines stretching as long as three hours at some locations. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy warned Sunday that staffing shortages could worsen later in the week, and union officials said mass resignations may be imminent.

Homan said ICE agents, deployed at a minimum of 50 personnel per shift at each airport, would not be performing screening duties such as operating magnetometers or X-ray machines, tasks for which they are not trained. Instead, officials said the agents would assist with crowd control, monitoring lines, and checking IDs at security checkpoint entrances and exits. Trump said Monday the deployment was his idea and that its primary mission was to help move passengers through security lines.

The presence of immigration agents near TSA checkpoints has unsettled travelers and drawn criticism from airport and airline workers, who expressed concern that officers unfamiliar with the customer-service demands of airport environments could create additional disruption. Richard Gennetti of the American Federation of Government Employees, which represents TSA officers, said the union opposed the deployment, calling it inappropriate and warning it could intimidate the public. A pilot and flight attendants on a Memphis-to-Atlanta flight, overheard by NBC News, expressed similar concern, saying they hoped ICE agents would not generate more chaos given what they described as a lack of the patience required in the industry. Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson said his office was monitoring federal officer deployments at O'Hare.
